tweets.text:
The commentary from the ISM report didn't reflect the strong metrics: 

“25 percent of the comments were positive and 69 percent negative, with a 1-to-2.7 ratio of positive to negative sentiment. Among comments, the Iran war was mentioned in 42 percent and tariffs in 18 percent; 57 percent of the panelists mentioned pricing volatility as an issue.” 

Every single comment referenced higher prices but one (Transportation) which referenced supply chain constraints. In that regard a notable comment was from electrical equipment noting memory chip volatility, “increased gas prices and tarris are causing long lead constraints and price hikes that customers are not willing to bear. Panic is starting within our industry.”

A couple of mentions of stronger than expected demand but also a couple of weakening, but the overwhelming focus of the comments was prices and supply chains.

https://t.co/3YD6RO3FZM
tweets.quoted_tweet_text: The % of manufacturing GDP that contracted fell sharply to just 2% (from 19% in April and 16% in March), while the share in strong contraction (PMI at or below 45, “a good barometer of overall manufacturing weakness”) also remained at 2% (same as April, down from 4% in March and https://t.co/mqFRwhnMbU

tweets.text:
From the Week Ahead (https://t.co/dK8IKSbarp):

Looking at potential vol control buying/selling this week, things are more favorable, although still just one 1%+ day falling off the lookbacks (still more than last week). 

The average for the rest of the days though on the 3-month lookback (the “trigger” given 1-month realized volatility remains under 3-month), is 0.78% up from 0.45% the prior week and 0.48% on the 1-month (from 0.2% the prior week).
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
From the Week Ahead (https://t.co/97EfWsKQm4):

BoA notes that systematic positioning has moved to new highs post-Iran conflict start, and they continue to see us with systematic flows having limited upside (in fact they see selling in all price paths), with big potential https://t.co/OdPx9YFuqq

tweets.text:
AI Winners, Token Economics, and Venture Strategy w/ David Clark and David George

VenCap CIO David Clark joins a16z GP David George to discuss the AI supercycle and how it's changing the technology industry, including what bigger exits mean for venture capital, why we're not in a bubble, why we're still early in enterprise AI adoption, and more.

00:00 Intro
00:38 The scale shift: Anthropic & OpenAI adding more revenue than hyperscalers
04:20 Skeuomorphic vs native AI applications in the enterprise
06:24 How the best AI companies run themselves differently
08:14 Top 1% exits 10x'd in 24 months
11:17 The half-life problem: Why 40% of AI leaders drop off every year
13:11 Token economics, cost pressure, and who captures value
17:00 Loss ratios, risk, and how to think about early stage
22:51 Are we in an AI bubble?
29:50 The future of venture capital in an AI world

@daveclark85 @DavidGeorge83
tweets.full_text:

tweets.text:
American defense companies are proving that operations can run at "machine speed".

The Pentagon hasn’t yet figured out how to support them in the field at the same tempo.

In fact, past automation efforts went poorly and ended up actually needing more people.

That’s obviously a big problem, but the alternative – not automating sustainment at all – locks us into a model that will surely lead to failure in the next conflict.

a16z's @JohnAguillard and @rmcentush on the sustainment bottleneck in autonomous warfare and how to fix it: https://t.co/HX9wp7bA8f

tweets.text:
YOU CAN'T HATE THE LEGACY MEDIA ENOUGH

This is one of the dumbest analysis you will ever see.

​The headline screams that AI is "7x the price." The article literally never explains what that 7x actually measures. 

Is it upfront hardware CapEx? Server power draw? Per-query operating costs? Scary number + zero definition = classic misinfo.

I guess they are trying to compare the infrastructure cost of AI queries to traditional search. 

But that comparison is entirely broken: legacy data centers provide ZERO intelligence. They just match keywords to a static index of links. You still have to do 100% of the reading, filtering, and thinking.

​An AI data center costs more to build and run because it is executing real-time matrix multiplication, reasoning, and data synthesis. It's an apple-to-computers comparison.

Let's ​look at the real unit economics of information, human vs AI: 

Buy a 500-page academic textbook for $100, and you are paying roughly $375 to $400 per million tokens for human-packaged content.

​Having a flagship AI model process, read, and summarize that exact same volume of information costs about $2.50. On a flash model, it costs $0.09.

So AI is driving a 97.5% to 99.9% hyper-deflationary collapse (a 2 to 3 order-of-magnitude drop) in the cost of active reasoning.

​if this trash article is referring to the capex (remember, they don't say), they can't just completely ignore a 150x to 4,400x collapse in operational unit economics.

​@Telegraph optimizes for clickbait because they know legacy media is structurally overpriced in a world of near-zero-cost intelligence.
 
They're terrified.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
'The free lunch is over. Google says demand has risen sevenfold over the past year. But now the real cost of AI is finally beginning to emerge, and the consequences are cataclysmic' I Writes @AndrewOrlowski

'The AI bubble will burst, and how that will happen is becoming https://t.co/B0Ke5iJ86f

thread_tweets.text: I'm not sure where Yardeni is getting that...Looks like U.S. MSCI. S&P 500 LT (3-5 yr - unspecified) analyst estimate is at 16%, but I don't trust those numbers as much b/c there are very few analysts who make that estimate. On a yearly basis for S&P 500, the numbers are much more reasonable and reflect a capex boom over next 18 months and then some normalization.

tweets.text:
Voters in seven states are heading to the polls on Tuesday, June 2nd to set the stage for the November general elections. If you are tracking the balance of power in Washington, tomorrow night will provide some massive clues.

Here is what you need to watch for:

🌴 California: The main event of the night. California uses a "top-two" primary, meaning the two candidates with the most votes advance to November, regardless of party. With control of the House on the line, these congressional races are absolutely critical.

🗽 New Jersey & 🌽 Iowa: Traditional partisan primaries that will lock in the final matchups for several highly competitive U.S. House seats.

🏜️ New Mexico, 🏔️ Montana, 🌻 South Dakota, & 🦅 Mississippi: Voters will be deciding on party nominees for their congressional delegations, state executive offices, and state legislatures.

Here is a look at today's @Kalshi most active election markets on @TheTerminal (so far):

tweets.text:
Good Morning from Germany, where more and more German citizens are leaving the country.
Last year, 288,579 Germans moved abroad, up from 270,000 the year before – a fresh record. The number of Germans moving back to Germany rose slightly, from 189,000 to 192,000. But the net balance still worsened to almost -97,000. That is the 2nd-highest net outflow of German citizens in the country’s history. Only 2016 was worse, at -135,000.
tweets.full_text:
Good Morning from Germany, where more and more German citizens are leaving the country.
Last year, 288,579 Germans moved abroad, up from 270,000 the year before – a fresh record. The number of Germans moving back to Germany rose slightly, from 189,000 to 192,000. But the net balance still worsened to almost -97,000. That is the 2nd-highest net outflow of German citizens in the country’s history. Only 2016 was worse, at -135,000.

It's not greed. It's not just Big Tech. 

The real cause has a name.

thread_tweets.text:
Profits and paychecks come out of the same revenue pie.

From 1970 to 2000, worker pay was 64% of business revenue. 

Today it's 54%. 

A 10-point slice of the economy moved from wages to profits.

4/ https://t.co/zjXkMw9w2E

thread_tweets.text:

The records are everywhere: manufacturing margins hit 15% in 2022 and sit near 13% today, the highest in 25 years.

Real private-sector income is now below its long-term trend, and the gap is widening.

That widening gap is the poor sentiment every consumer survey keeps picking up.

thread_tweets.text:
The proof shows it isn't greed or offshoring: the median firm saw no margin improvement at all. 

Only the superstar firms did. 

Margins even rose in services, where offshoring doesn't have a direct impact.

It's about market concentration. Everywhere.

8/
thread_tweets.full_text:
The proof shows it isn't greed or offshoring: the median firm saw no margin improvement at all. 

Only the superstar firms did. 

Margins even rose in services, where offshoring doesn't have a direct impact.

It's about market concentration. Everywhere.

8/

thread_tweets.text:
The labor share didn't fall because companies cut pay. 

It fell because the work moved to giant companies with more scale. 

Wages still rose, just slower than revenue. 

Pay shrank as a share of the pie, and profit took the difference.

7/

How did this happen?

A National Bureau of Economic Research paper "The Fall of the Labor Share and the Rise of Superstar Firms" helps with the answer.

It's not corporate greed. 

6/
